coredata.dc:title: Portable smartphone-integrated AuAg nanoclusters electrospun membranes for multivariate fluorescent sensing of Hg2+, Cu2+ and l-histidine in water and food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Hongbo
Xie, Ruyan
Hao, Yunqi
Pang, Jingyu
Gao, Hong
Qu, Fengyu
Tian, Miaomiao
Guo, Changhong
Mao, Baodong
Chai, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Detection of heavy metals have been pivotal due to their non-biodegradability and food chain accumulation. Herein, a multivariate ratiometric sensor was developed by in situ integrating AuAg nanoclusters (NCs) into electrospun cellulose acetate nanofibrous membranes (AuAg-ENM) for visual detection of Hg2+, Cu2+ and consecutive sensing of l-histidine (His), which was integrated into a smartphone platform for quantitative on-site detection. AuAg-ENM achieved multivariate detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by fluorescence quenching, and subsequent selective recovery of the Cu2+-quenched fluorescence by His, which distinguished Hg2+ and Cu2+ and fulfilled determination of His simultaneously. Notably, AuAg-ENM achieved selective monitoring of Hg2+, Cu2+ and His in water, food and serum samples with high accuracy comparable to ICP and HPLC tests. A logic gate circuit was devised to further explain and promote the application of AuAg-ENM detection by smartphone App. This portable AuAg-ENM provides a promising reference for fabricating intelligent visual sensors for multiple detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AuAg nanoclusters
Electrospun nanofibrous membrane
Multivariate detection
Hg2+
Cu2+
l -histidine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and performance of AuAg-ENM sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was ﬁnancially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (22171060), Excellent Scientific Research Team Project of Harbin Normal University (XKYT202001), Graduate Innovation Fund of Harbin Normal University (HSDBSCX2017, HSDBSCX2022-14).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of AuAgNCs (inset: HRTEM, size distribution of AuAgNCs). (b) The fluorescence spectra under different excitation wavelength (inset: 3-D fluorescent contour). (c) SEM (inset: the size distribution of shells). (d) Fluorescent micrographs of AuAg-ENM. (e) Images of AuAgNCs mixed with various metal ions under a UV lamp. (f) Colorimetric fluorescent response of AuAg-ENM to various metal ions.

Fluorescence spectra of AuAgNCs with different concentrations (1–1000 nM) of (a) Hg2+ and (e) Cu2+, the calibration plot between current response (I/I0) and the concentrations of (b) Hg2+ and (f) Cu2+, corresponding images (c) Hg2+ and (g) Cu2+, AuAg-ENM with (d) Hg2+ and (h) Cu2+.

The visual-detection and corresponding linear relationship between the value R and the concentration (1–1000 μM) of (a) Hg2+ and (c) Cu2+ by ratiometric AuAg-ENM, and plot of color variation of R versus concentrations (10–100 μM) of (b) Hg2+ and (d) Cu2+.
(a) Selectivity evaluation for His by AuAg-ENM + Hg2+ (up) and AuAg-ENM + Cu2+ (bottom)). (b) Sensitivity for His and (c) linear relationship between R value and concentration of His.
(a) The logic gate scheme, (b) truth tables of Hg2+, Cu2+ and His. (c) The histogram of the relative value of R channel with different concentration of Cu2+, Hg2+ and (d) His.

Schematic illustration of the fabrication of AuAg-ENM and the sensing process (Step 1: Synthesis of AuAgNCs; Step 2: ENM integrated AuAgNCs by electrospinning; Step 3: Visual detection by AuAg-ENM assisted by a smartphone App).

head.ce:title: Portable smartphone-integrated AuAg nanoclusters electrospun membranes for multivariate fluorescent sensing of Hg2+, Cu2+ and l-histidine in water and food samples

College of Life Science and Technology

Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the possible mechanism for selectivity by assistant of His. (b) The practical sensing performance in food and water detection.

Detection of heavy metals have been pivotal due to their non-biodegradability and food chain accumulation. Herein, a multivariate ratiometric sensor was developed by in situ integrating AuAg nanoclusters (NCs) into electrospun cellulose acetate nanofibrous membranes (AuAg-ENM) for visual detection of Hg2+, Cu2+ and consecutive sensing of l-histidine (His), which was integrated into a smartphone platform for quantitative on-site detection. AuAg-ENM achieved multivariate detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by fluorescence quenching, and subsequent selective recovery of the Cu2+-quenched fluorescence by His, which distinguished Hg2+ and Cu2+ and fulfilled determination of His simultaneously. Notably, AuAg-ENM achieved selective monitoring of Hg2+, Cu2+ and His in water, food and serum samples with high accuracy comparable to ICP and HPLC tests. A logic gate circuit was devised to further explain and promote the application of AuAg-ENM detection by smartphone App. This portable AuAg-ENM provides a promising reference for fabricating intelligent visual sensors for multiple detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution has been one of the most strictly controlled items for their non-biodegradability, gradually accumulation in the food chain, and subsequent crucial environmental and health problems (Liu et al., 2022). Among them, Hg2+ is one of the most toxic that poses a serious threat to both human life and natural environment, whereas excessive uptake of Cu2+ can also cause various severe diseases and disorders (Wang et al., 2019). The safety limits of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in drinking water are 10 nM and 20 μM, respectively, according to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (Fang et al., 2010). It is critical to develop sensitive and accurate methods for detecting Hg2+ and Cu2+ to ensure the safety of the environment, food and human body. Until today, conventional techniques, such as the atomic adsorption spectrometry (Logan et al., 2020), remain the standard method for quantification of heavy metal ions, but they cannot meet the needs of fast on-site detection and monitoring (Londonio et al., 2019). Nowdays, direct read-out, portable and mini-device-based methods have been a critical goal for heavy metal detection. Compared with traditional instrumental analyses, emerging optical and electronic sensors exhibited great advantage on portability, speed, and simplicity. Considerable efforts have been contributed to the development of colorimetric, fluorescent and electronic sensors (Shen et al., 2022). Among them, the fluorescent probes have attracted widespread attention by using various efficient and easy manipulating fluorescent materials, such as semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots and Au nanoclusters (Liu et al., 2020).

AuNCs are a large class of atomically-precise nanostructures with core size less than 3 nm, and have been considered as a competent area of study (Kang et al., 2020), owing to their unique structure-dependent fluorescent nature towards changes in the microenvironment (Bian et al., 2017). Till now, AuNCs have been widely explored in analyzing heavy metals in the environment and foods, due to their tunable and easily modified fluorescence properties during or after the synthesis (Nasaruddin et al., 2018). Tremendous efforts have been contributed to the synthesis and modification of AuNCs as new fluorescent probes for sensing heavy metals (Yu et al., 2018). The challenge on high selectivity, easy on-site operation and multivariate detection still existed due to complicated environment.

For practical applications in on-site test, the convenient storage, portability and operation are crucial factors beyond performance. A key significance is sought towards practical applications in the adaptation of the Au NC probes in various solid matrix such as MOFs, SiO2 and electrospun membrane (ENM) (Li et al., 2021, Zhou et al., 2019). Among them, the ENM have been widely explored for the immobilization of AuNCs and exhibited excellent performance owing to their advantages of large specific surface area, easy modification, and high flexibility (Chen and Jiang, 2021). The incorporation of active probes into ENM matrix plays a key role in analysis, since the Au NCs precursors are chemically cross linked to polymers and used for the fabrication of ENM (Zhang et al., 2020). It can not only faithfully transduce the sensing behavior of AuNCs, but also highlight convenient portability and storage (Zhang et al., 2017). For example, Senthamizhan et al. demonstrated the high performance of AuNCs decorated polycaprolactone ENM for real time visual monitoring of Hg2+ detection at ppt level in water (Senthamizhan et al., 2014). Therefore, it has been one of the most powerful methods to embedding AuNCs and reproduce their unique sensing properties on ENM.

Moreover, multifunctional detection is an urgent need to monitor heavy metal pollution, due to most of the coexistence of different metal ions in natural waters. It is of great significance to develop multifunctional probes for determining two or more ions by different signals with satisfied selectivity in a complex environment, which could greatly improve the cost and time. Great efforts have been exploited to the design of multifunctional probes for heavy metal sensing. Yu et al. has developed a multi-colorful ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe composed of AuNCs and CDs for fluorescent sensing Hg2+ and Cr6+ simultaneously and separately (Yu et al., 2018). However, it is still a great challenge to assure high selectivity during the multivariate detection. Therefore, the research of low cost, good selectivity for multiple sensing method should be encouraged greatly.

Smartphone-assisted detections by using color recognizing Apps have been reported for sensing of heavy metal ions, which promotes the development of on-site/real time detection and exhibited superior advantages in simplifying the process and saving equipment cost (Chu et al., 2020). The multiple sensing and discrimination for heavy metal ions in complicated environment by smartphone platform is still urgent to explore.

Herein, a portable smartphone platform integrated AuAgNCs enabled ENM sensor was fabricated for visual multi-sensing of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by ratiometric fluorescent quenching and His’ recovering “on–off-on” process. Subsequently, the sensor performed reliable monitoring of Hg2+ and Cu2+ with low LOD down to 12.36 and 25.90 nM. The AuAgNCs enabled the AuAg-ENM strong red emission, which possess the same function for recognizing Hg2+ and Cu2+ by fluorescence quenching. The natural blue emitting background of the ENM enables the assembly of a ratiometric probe with red fluorescence AuAgNCs, which accomplishes a characteristic fluorescence color evolution from red to purple and blue on the AuAg-ENM by quenching the red emission of AuAgNCs. Moreover, introducing His can recover the quenched fluorescence by Cu2+, not Hg2+, which can distinguish Hg2+ and Cu2+ in a multivariate detection, and consecutive detecting His. All above mentioned detection process can be achieved on a smartphone App rapidly and simply. The study illustrated several highlights including (1) multivariate detection of Hg2+, Cu2+ and His by AuAg-ENM synchronously, (2) convenient and real-time/on-site detection, (3) fabricating and fulfilling of the portable smartphone readout-based ratiometric sensing system. The proposed sensing strategy provides a favorable readout means for on-site and rapid detection, and establishes a reliable and convenient point-of-care testing platform.

The synthesis of AuAgNCs was performed according to previous literature with minor improvement (Zhou et al., 2016). Briefly, BSA (5 mL, 50 mg/mL) was incubated with HAuCl4 (5 mL, 10 mM) in a 37 °C water bath for 5 min under vigorous stirring. Then, 0.5 mL of NaOH solution (1 M) was introduced, and kept stirring for 12 h at 37 °C. After that, 2.5 mL of AgNO3 solution (10 mM) was poured in and mixed for 5 min, where 50 μL of NaOH solution (1 M) was added and the mixture was remained stirring at 37 °C for 24 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the resultant AuAgNCs were obtained by dialysis purifying of 8000 Da for 24 h and stored at 4 °C.

The clear and homogenous cellulose acetate (CA) solution was prepared in a mixture of acetic acid and AuAgNCs aqueous solution (3:1, v/v) system, using 5 % wt% of CA (Fig. S1, S2). The CA solutions were taken up in a 5 mL syringe fitted with a metallic needle of 0.65 mm inner diameter. The electrospinning process was performed at 25 °C. Electrospinning parameters were adjusted as follows: feed rate of solutions = 0.5 mL/h, applied voltage = 15 kV, and tip-to-collector distance = 15 cm.

The process of detection includes five sections: (1) detection by AuAgNCs solution, (2) detection by AuAg-ENM, (3) AuAg-ENM smartphone-detection-system, (4) Analysis in real water samples, and (5) Detection His by AuAg-ENM + Cu2+ system. All these processes have been illustrated in supporting information. Human blood samples were collected from healthy adult volunteers and treated according to the NIH Guidelines for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals (Albus, 2012). This work has received approval for research ethics from the Ethics Committee of Harbin Normal University.

The ratiometric AuAg-ENM was feasible for tracing Hg2+ and Cu2+ based on fluorescence quenching, distinguishing Cu2+ from Hg2+, and consecutive monitoring His by fluorescence recovery. As demonstrated in Scheme 1 , AuAgNCs can be utilized for sensing of Hg2+ and Cu2+ via “on–off” switch, the performance can be extended the AuAg-ENM. As portrayed in Step 2, the original CA-ENM exhibited a blue background under UV-lamp, whereas the AuAg-ENM emitted red fluorescence under UV-lamp. The natural blue substrate provides a condition for assembling a ratiometric probe, which enables a rich fluorescent color adjustability from red to purple and blue on the AuAg-ENM. More importantly, the detection of AuAg-ENM can be duplicated veritably, ensured reliable monitoring of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by reading out the color gradient (Step 3 of Scheme 1). The final target is to measure the heavy metals by the Color Grab on a smartphone to recognize the corresponding red (R) values to accomplish quantitative detection (Fig. S3). The quenching fluorescence of AuAg-ENM by Hg2+ and Cu2+ further treated by exposure to His, subsequently the fluorescence of strips quenched by Cu2+, providing an “off–on” switching, but Hg2+ could not. The introduce of His not only provides a strategy to distinguish Hg2+ and Cu2+, but also gives a signal to monitor His quantitatively. Thus, the AuAg-ENM provides a favorable ratiometric platform for reliable and multivariate on-site sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and performance of AuAg-ENM sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and optical properties of AuAgNCs were characterized by TEM and fluorescence spectra, respectively. TEM images show spherical particles with an average diameter of 3.60 ± 0.03 nm (Fig. 1 a). The HRTEM image of AuAgNCs (inset of Fig. 1a) exhibited two clear lattice fringes of 0.225 and 0.237 nm, which matched well with the (111) plane of phase Au, and (111) plane of cubic phaseAg, confirming the components of AuAgNCs (Hu et al., 2020). The UV–vis spectrum showed a strong absorption band at 218 nm and a shoulder peak at 280 nm (Fig. S4) can be ascribed to the n-π* transition. The fluorescence emission was located at 625 nm with a shoulder peak at 655 nm under the excitation of 370 nm (Fig. S4), which can also be confirmed by the CIE (inset of Fig. 1b). The fluorescence excitation independence of the AuAgNCs was also proved by testing excitations ranging from 340 to 440 nm (Fig. 1b).

The morphology of AuAg-ENM was measured by SEM and fluorescent optical microscope. As demonstrated in Fig. 1c, AuAg-ENM exhibited uniform micro hollow hemisphere structure, which was different from typical electrospun nanofibers (Kim et al., 2021). The diameters of the hemispheres were distributed between 2 and 4 μm with an average size of 3.09 ± 0.06 μm (inset of Fig. 1c), and a thickness shell of 200 nm, which benefits to adsorbing more AuAgNCs and increasing their density, improving the performance of detection.

The fluorescent micrographs of AuAg-ENM were also taken to verify the incorporation of AuAgNCs and CA-ENM, which endowed AuAg-ENM sensing functionality by introducing functional groups, such as amino, sulfhydryl and phosphate groups on the surface of composite ENM. As demonstrated in Fig. 1d, the intense red fluorescence of the AuAgNCs was observed, indicating the uniform distribution of AuAgNCs inside the CA-ENM. The hollow micro-hemispheres can be clearly observed from the image (Fig. 1d), ensuring the stable fluorescence of ENM due to the hollow micro-hemispheres enlarged the surface of ENM.

To verify the sensing behavior of the proposed AuAg-ENM, selectivity was first evaluated by investigating the relevant metal ions. Fig. S5a illustrates the fluorescence spectra of the AuAgNCs solution with various metal ions (100 μM). The fluorescence was eliminated by Hg2+ and Cu2+, whereas other samples remained nearly unchanged (Fig. S5b). The results were agreed with corresponding photo images (Fig. 1e), demonstrating the high selectivity of AuAgNCs probe. Further the stability of AuAgNCs, the selectivity with various pH was carried out and the results (Fig. S6, S7) manifested the well pH tolerance of probe (Mahmoodi et al., 2019). The interference of detection was further evaluated by estimating the detection of Hg2+ co-existence of the various cations (Fig. S8-S10), the results indicated the excellent anti-interference of AuAgNCs probe. The excellent stability of AuAg-ENM was also confirmed by storage (Fig. S11) and ions leaching experiment (Fig. S12, Table S1). When the detection was extended to AuAg-ENM stripes (Fig. 1f), Hg2+ and Cu2+ caused recognizable color changes from red to purple by quenching the red emission of AuAg-ENM. This phenomenon can be observed by naked eyes and purveyed a convenient and precise detection method.

The response of AuAgNCs to different concentrations of Hg2+ was also evaluated, the results manifested fluorescent intensity was closely related to Hg2+ concentration (Fig. 2 a). And a linear correlation (R2 = 0.996) in the range of 10–100 nM was established by plotting I/I0 (I: samples, I0: blank) versus the concentration of Hg2+ (Fig. 2b inset), corresponding images were also demonstrated FL quenching with Hg2+ addition (Fig. 2c). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 2.45 nM according to 3σ/k (Bian et al., 2017), which was lower than the EPA standard of the drinking water (Yu et al., 2018). Remarkably, the sensing behavior of AuAg-ENM outperformed the solution probe, exhibiting effective colorimetric erosion on the strips (Fig. 2d) with a clear color evolution from red to purple and blue, and the concentration of Hg2+ can be discriminated clearly.

The visual detection of Cu2+ was similar to the phenomenon of Hg2+. The fluorescence intensity of AuAgNCs probe was attenuated with the continuous increased of Cu2+ (Fig. 2e), where the curve of I/I0 and concentration of Cu2+ (10–50 nM) (Fig. 2f) exhibited good linear correlation with LOD of 1.92 nM (R2 = 0.997), far below the EPA safety limit of Cu2+ in drinking water (Tang et al., 2020). The corresponding colorimetric probe and strip images were displayed at Fig. 2g-2 h, where the fluorescence quenching was associated with concentration of Cu2+ closely.

Nevertheless, the color recognition is often limited to semiquantitative monitoring and cannot acquire high accuracy due to the difficulty to distinguish a slight color variation by naked eyes owing to the vision tiredness. Thus, a smartphone-based analysis system is inevitable for precise detection by reading out the RGB data with Color Grab.

The detection supported by smartphone can be performed easily. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, ratiometric color evolved from red to oxblood red, purple, blue, navy blue with increasing concentration of Hg2+ (up inset). Compared with the color evolution of AuAgNCs (Fig. 2c), the single-color difference related to the degree of quenching is very difficult to identify by naked eyes. In contrast, the colorimetric strips displayed ratiometric colors suitable for visual detection, in which color transformation was observable with the addition of 1 μM Hg2+ since human eyes are more sensitive to color variations (Fig. 3). The specific RGB analysis based on the Color Recognizer App exhibit evident monitoring effect with the concentration of Hg2+ (Fig. 3a). An excellent linear correlation between values of R and concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 1–100 μM (R2 = 0.998) (Fig. 3b). And the LOD of AuAg-ENM can be determined at 12.36 nM, which enabled portable semiquantitative detection of Hg2+ by a smartphone. The facile AuAg-ENM strips-based analysis operation can be applied for real time and on-site monitoring the Hg2+ in environment water.

The fluorescence color of the AuAg-ENM strips changed from purple to blue with Cu2+ higher than 6 μM (Fig. 3c, 3d), which expressed a distinct ratiometric color evolution suitable for quantitative determination of Cu2+ within a wide range from 1 to 100 μM. The LOD of Cu2+ by AuAg-ENM strips was calculated to 25.90 nM, which was commendable to the standard of EPA (Senthamizhan et al., 2015). The recent reported methods for detection of Hg2+, Cu2+ were listed in the Table S2. In comparison, both Au/Ag nanoclusters and nanocluster based hybrid probe exhibited excellent sensitivity. Our methods are comparative even better than the reported probe (Table S2) (Gokul Eswaran et al., 2021, Tan et al., 2020), indicating its great potential for sensitive and precise detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ with a simple manipulation.

The high selectivity is necessary in a multivariate detection. Discrimination of between Hg2+ and Cu2+ can be solved by further treatment. After sensing Hg2+ and Cu2+, the AuAg-ENM strips were exposed to the common biological molecules including His, GSH, BSA, Cys, Met, GA, Trp, AA, DA and GLU (Fig. 4 a). Interestingly, only His recovered the fluorescence of AuAg-ENM + Cu2+, not Hg2+, and others remained no change. The recovered fluorescence by His enables the distinguishment of Hg2+ and Cu2+ of AuAg-ENM, endowing the simultaneous multivariate detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+. The recovered fluorescence was related to the concentration of His closely, promotes the monitoring His by enhancement of fluorescence of AuAg-ENM (Fig. 4b). This sensing performance was further confirmed by AuAgNCs, indicated the consistent reliable results (Fig. S12). Moreover, the selective recognition of His within other small biological molecules can also be confirmed by both AuAgNCs (Fig. S14, S15). A good linear relationship of R value with His concentration was determined in the range of 10 to 100 μM with a LOD of 16.60 nM (Fig. 4c). The AuAg-ENM + Cu2+ stripes can consecutively trace His through an “off–on” switching with a LOD as low as 16.60 nM, which achieved multivariant visual detection with high selectivity of Hg2+, Cu2+ and His.

Feasibility of AuAg-ENM was further evaluated by detecting Hg2+ and Cu2+ in real samples. The recovery rates in water by monitoring of Hg2+ and Cu2+ using AuAg-ENM strips were calculated to be 89.5 %-113.0 % and 80.7 %-102.8 % respectively, with the relative standard deviations (RSD) less than 4.0 % (Table S3). Notably, the same samples detected by ICP-OES were parallel to the data tested by AuAg-ENM with a relative error of 0.8 % and 2.0 %, confirming their accuracy of sensing and environmental tolerance. Known quantities of Hg2+ and Cu2+ were spiked into food samples (Section 1.44 in supplementary material) to test the practicability of AuAg-ENM, the results (Table S4) demonstrated recoveries were 86.7 ∼ 103.3 % (Hg2+) and 95.44 ∼ 104.1 % (Cu2+), and the relative standard deviations (n = 3) were less than 5 %, manifested this method can be highly reproducible and accurate for screening of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in real samples. Moreover, it is of vital significance in pharmaceutical research detection of His in serum, because the absence or excess of His may cause severe diseases, such as chronic kidney diseases and Parkinson's diseases (Tan et al., 2020). The recovery of His in serum was also evaluated and calculated at 96.3 %∼101.7 % with RSD less than 4.0 %. Satisfactorily, the results obtained by HPLC in the same serum samples were comparable to the data tested by AuAg-ENM with a relative error of 1.7 %, demonstrating the reliability of AuAg-ENM strips for determining His level in humoral samples (Table S5). The acceptable recoveries manifested the applicability of the AuAg-ENM strips for detection of Hg2+, Cu2+ and His in real samples. And the multivariate detection mode based “on–off-on” switch may be further explored to meet the needs of various heavy metal pollution detection and biological markers.

Since AuAg-ENM can effectively achieve multivariate detection of Hg2+, Cu2+ and His by on–off-on switch of fluorescence, three-input concatenated logic circuits were constructed by combining the visual fluorescence sensing with intelligent molecular computing. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the Hg2+ concentration (50–1000 μM) was defined as input signal “A”, the Cu2+ concentration (100–1000 μM) as input “B”, and the range of His (10–1000 μM) as input “C”, respectively. The inputs are coded as “1” when they are present and “0” when they are absent. The normalized fluorescence intensity histogram of AuAg-ENM were triggered by His. And, for Cu2+, Hg2+ and His, the color values of AuAg-ENM strips below and above the threshold value of 0.54, 0.55 and 0.86 are set as 0 and 1, corresponding the color of blue and red, respectively (Fig. 5c and Fig. 5d). The output of red channel is (0), which means the red fluorescence is remained “on”, and output (1) is blue channel, indicating the red fluorescence is shut off.

The truth table of three logic gate decoder are given in Fig. 5b, displaying the fluorescence color change of AuAg-ENM under various input conditions. Without (A) Hg2+ and (B) Cu2+, the fluorescence is red and the output is (0). In the presence of either Hg2+ or Cu2+, input A or B results in the output of (1), the red fluorescence quenching, displayed blue color. With input A and C, the output is (1), e.g., the fluorescence quenched by Hg2+ could not be recovered by His. With input B and C, the output is (0), manifesting His can turn on the red fluorescence of AuAg-ENM quenched by Cu2+. To conclude, this logic gate function based on the concentration and color changes is expected to be used for multivariate detection of Hg2+, Cu2+ and His.

As anticipated, the AuAg-ENM strips exhibited satisfied sensitivity and selectivity for multivariate detection, which can be ascribed to the following aspects. The surface-initiated polymerization produced ENM with chemically crosslinked AuAgNCs, in which the hollow hemisphere morphology of ENM enlarged the surface area, enhanced distribution of AuAgNCs on ENM surface and subsequently improved the sensitivity and responding time (Fig. S1a). For selectivity of Cu2+ vs Hg2+, the AuAgNCs in ENMs experienced an unrecoverable quenching of fluorescence within 5 min owing to the formation of AuAg-Hg amalgam (Senthamizhan et al., 2015). However, Cu2+ binds with the AuAgNCs to form a metal–ligand complex, which could be replaced by His easily by forming Cu-His composites (Gonzalez et al., 2019), triggering different phenomena in detecting Cu2+ and Hg2+. The His-enabled “on–off-on” mode achieves high selectivity and multivariate detection by the AuAgNCs system. Further, the quenching mechanism in detection process was evaluated. It can be observed (Fig. S16a) that a remarkable overlap occurred between the fluorescence spectrum of AuAgNCs (donor) and UV–vis absorption spectra of Hg2+ and Cu2+ (acceptors) respectively, indicating the internal filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) may exist in the process of detection. Meanwhile, the significant decrease of fluorescence lifetime of AuAgNCs appeared after the addition of the acceptors, confirming the presence of FRET, because the stability of fluorescence lifetime of donor was a necessary precondition to cause the IFE (Yang et al., 2020). Thus, AuAgNCs can theoretically form effective FRET donor–acceptor pairs with Hg2+ and Cu2+. Also, the fluorescence lifetime of the donor evidently decreased with the existence of acceptor, implying the dynamic quenching effect (DQE) occurred between donor–acceptor pairs (Fig. S16b). The fluorescence lifetime of donor decreased from 14.9 ms to 7.4 ms and 7.3 ms, when 100 μM Hg2+ and Cu2+ were added into the AuAgNCs, respectively. And the transfer efficiency of FRET was calculated to be 50.3 % and 47.0 % independently, according to the equation:
η
FRET
=
τ
DA
/
τ D
(τ D : lifetime of donor, τ DA : lifetime of donor–acceptor pairs), which is beneficial to establish the sensing system (Shen et al., 2022). All of these results provided a convenience for the portable and on-site detection by smartphone-based sensing platform, which would be a useful reference for construction of similar sensing systems.

In summary, a ratiometric fluorescent system was developed by embedding AuAgNCs in ENM for multivariate rapid visual detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ with the potential of smartphone-enabled real-time/on-site application. Based on the AuAgNCs enabled hollow hemisphere shaped ENM, the multivariate detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ was achieved by His-attained “on–off-on” strategy with high accuracy and selectivity. The consecutive detection of His was also accomplished via restoring the fluorescence of AuAg-ENM quenched by Cu2+, which is the same process for selective recognizing Hg2+ and Cu2+. The low detection limit (Hg2+: 12.36 nM, Cu2+: 25.90 nM) combines with high selectivity of the AuAg-ENM system renders multiple safety monitoring and control of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in drinking water., which was confirmed in real environmental water and serum samples with convenient operation on a smartphone. Accordingly, a logic gate circuit was devised, proving the application potential of integrating the AuAg-ENM system in intelligent devices. The AuAg-ENM system not only provides a useful platform for smartphone-assisted multivariate visual detection in environmental detection, but also broadens the design and construction of multifunctional sensors via delicate manipulating the metal NCs and ENM.
Credit Author Statement

Hongbo Wu: Experimental implementation, Data organization. Ruyan Xie: Data organization, First draft of manuscript writing. Yunqi Hao: Data organization, Repeat the experiment. Jingyu Pang: Data organization. Mingyu Tang: Experimental data proofreading. Hong Gao: Experimental data proofreading. Fengyu Qu*: Data organization, Experimental design. Miaomiao Tian: Resources. Changhong Guo*: Experimental design, Resources. Baodong Mao: Experimental design, Data organization, Embellishment of manuscripts. Fang Chai: Project proposal design, experiment idea guidance, manuscript writing guidance, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was ﬁnancially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (22171060), Excellent Scientific Research Team Project of Harbin Normal University (XKYT202001), Graduate Innovation Fund of Harbin Normal University (HSDBSCX2017, HSDBSCX2022-14).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.135961.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
